---
title: "3.1 Abstraction and Program Design"
subtitle: "Class Creation"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit3-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[ŌåÉ Back to AP CSA / Ķ┐öÕø×Ķ»Šń©ŗńø«ÕĮĢ](../../index.html)

## Abstraction and Program Design

### Design classes before writing code

In Unit 1, students used built-in classes such as `String` and `Turtle`.  
This topic shifts to writing our own classes by planning:

::: {.tight-list}
- what <span class="term">objects</span> the program needs
- what <span class="term">attributes</span> each object should store
- what <span class="term">behaviors</span> each object should provide
:::

## Object-Oriented Design

### Start from nouns, then map data and methods

In <span class="term">object-oriented design</span>, programmers identify likely classes from the problem description.

::: {.compare-grid}
::: {.soft-box}
**Look for nouns**

- `Student`
- `Course`
- `Turtle`
- `World`
:::
::: {.soft-box}
**Then plan**

- data to track in each class
- methods each class should provide
- how the classes work together
:::
:::

Students should treat nouns as class candidates and verbs as clues for methods.

## UML Class Diagrams

### A diagram can show the class at a glance

![UML class diagram for Turtle](../../assets/3-class-creation/3-1-abstraction-and-program-design/turtleUMLClassDiagram.png){fig-align="center" width="74%"}

::: {.tight-list}
- <span class="term">Class diagrams</span> summarize a class without showing implementation details
- attributes stand for the data in the class
- methods stand for the behaviors in the class
- `-` means private and `+` means public, but detailed UML syntax is <span class="mark">not the AP CSA focus</span>
:::

## Abstraction

### Focus on the main idea and hide irrelevant detail

<span class="term">Abstraction</span> reduces complexity by keeping attention on the idea that matters right now.

::: {.tight-list}
- the Turtle diagram shows what the class contains
- it does <span class="mark">not</span> show how every method is implemented
- this makes planning and communication easier
:::

The class diagram itself is an example of abstraction.

## Data and Procedural Abstraction

### Manage complexity through names, structure, and reusable actions

::: {.compare-grid}
::: {.soft-box}
**Data abstraction**

- gives data a meaningful name
- separates what the data represents from its implementation
- includes attributes such as instance variables
- can include shared <span class="term">class variables</span>
:::
::: {.soft-box}
**Procedural abstraction**

- gives a process a method name
- lets us use a method without knowing every internal step
- supports <span class="term">method decomposition</span>
- improves reuse when methods accept parameters
:::
:::

Examples from earlier work include `forward()` on `Turtle` and song methods such as `chorus()`.

## Why Procedural Abstraction Matters

### Smaller methods make programs easier to build and revise

Retained reasons from the textbook:

::: {.tight-list}
- <span class="term">Organization</span>: divide a large problem into smaller parts
- <span class="term">Code reuse</span>: avoid repeating the same code
- <span class="term">Maintainability and debugging</span>: smaller methods are easier to trace and fix
- <span class="term">Flexibility</span>: parameters generalize one method for many inputs
:::

As long as the method signature and behavior stay consistent, the implementation can change internally.

## Classroom Tasks

### Practice worth keeping

Retained classroom work for this topic:

::: {.tight-list}
- identify likely classes from nouns in a problem description
- infer attributes and methods for sample classes such as `Student` and `Course`
- review procedural abstraction with method decomposition and parameters
- <span class="term">3.1.2 Coding Exercise: Old MacDonald Song Repeated</span>
- <span class="term">3.1.6 Group Challenge: Game Design</span>
:::

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should...

::: {.tight-list}
- explain how abstraction reduces complexity
- distinguish <span class="term">data abstraction</span> from <span class="term">procedural abstraction</span>
- identify possible classes from nouns in a specification
- describe how attributes and methods support class design
- explain why decomposition and parameters improve reuse
:::

## End

### Return to the course outline

[ŌåÉ Back to AP CSA / Ķ┐öÕø×Ķ»Šń©ŗńø«ÕĮĢ](../../index.html)
